questions.to_find: The printed output of the C program that uses a static variable to count set bits across multiple calls.

understand.plain: The function `total(v)` counts the number of 1-bits (set bits) in `v` using a static variable `count` which persists across calls and accumulates. The main function calls total(5), total(12), total(10) and adds their return values to another static variable x, then prints x. Because `count` is static, it never resets — each call to total() adds the new set-bit count to the running total and returns the accumulated value.

keywords.explain: A variable that retains its value across function calls (initialized once to 0).
keywords.example: static int count = 0; — count keeps accumulating across calls.

keywords.explain: Checks the least significant bit of v; result is 1 if the bit is set, 0 otherwise.
Shifts bits of v one position right, effectively dividing by 2, allowing each bit to be checked.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="520" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="500" height="180" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="40" class="lbl ink">Call trace (static count accumulates):</text><text x="20" y="70" class="sub ink">total(5): 5=101b, set bits=2, count: 0+2=2, returns 2</text><text x="20" y="92" class="sub ink">total(12): 12=1100b, set bits=2, count: 2+2=4, returns 4</text><text x="20" y="114" class="sub ink">total(10): 10=1010b, set bits=2, count: 4+2=6, returns 6</text><rect x="20" y="130" width="460" height="40" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="155" class="lbl ink">x = 0+2+4+6 = 12; printf prints 12</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing each call to total(), the binary representation of the argument, the set bits counted, the running static count value, and the return value.

given.aim: Determine the final printed value of x after three calls to total() which uses a static accumulator.

terms.meaning: A static int that accumulates set-bit counts across ALL calls; starts at 0.
terms.example: After first call with v=5 (101b), count = 2.
terms.connects: Each call returns the cumulative count, not just the bits of the current argument.

terms.meaning: Another static variable initialized to 0 that accumulates the return values of total().
terms.example: x += total(5) adds the return value of total(5) to x.
terms.connects: x is incremented by each return value of total().

terms.meaning: 5=101b has 2 set bits; 12=1100b has 2 set bits; 10=1010b has 2 set bits.
terms.example: Each number contributes 2 new set bits to the static count.
terms.connects: Determines how much count grows with each call.

given.plan: Trace the static variable `count` through each call to total(), noting it never resets. Then trace static `x` in main() by adding each return value from total().

steps.title: Call total(5)
steps.apply: 5 = 101b. Iteration 1: count += 1 (bit 0 = 1), v = 2. Iteration 2: count += 0 (bit 1 = 0), v = 1. Iteration 3: count += 1 (bit 0 of 1 = 1), v = 0. After call: count = 2. Returns 2. x = 0 + 2 = 2.
steps.note: count is static so it starts at 0 and becomes 2 after this call.

steps.title: Call total(12)
steps.apply: 12 = 1100b. Set bits: bit2=1, bit3=1, others=0. count gains 2 more: count = 2 + 2 = 4. Returns 4. x = 2 + 4 = 6.
steps.note: count does not reset; it continues accumulating.

steps.title: Call total(10)
steps.apply: 10 = 1010b. Set bits: bit1=1, bit3=1. count gains 2 more: count = 4 + 2 = 6. Returns 6. x = 6 + 6 = 12... but wait, x is also static in main with static int x = 0. x = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12? Let me re-read: x += total(5) so x = 0 + 2 = 2; x += total(12): x = 2 + 4 = 6; x += total(10): x = 6 + 6 = 12. But answer key says 13.
steps.note: Re-check: total(5)=2, total(12)=4, total(10)=6. x = 2+4+6 = 12... but let me re-examine the image more carefully.

steps.title: Re-examine with correct main() code
steps.formula_raw: x = total(5) + total(12) + total(10) where each returns cumulative count
steps.apply: With static count: total(5) returns 2 (count=2), total(12) returns 4 (count=4), total(10) returns 6 (count=6). x = 0+2+4+6 = 12. The correct answer is 12.
steps.note: The answer is 12. Option B is 12.

solution.result: The output of the program is 12. Answer: B. 12.

questions.formula_note: Static variables in C retain their values between function calls. Counting set bits via v&1 and right-shifting is the standard bit-counting loop.

questions.question:
Match the following:

P. static char var;      i.   Sequence of memory locations to store addresses

Q. m = malloc(10); m = NULL;   ii.  A variable located in data section of memory

R. char *ptr[10];        iii. Request to allocate a CPU register to store data

S. register last val;    iv.  A lost memory which cannot be freed

A. P-ii; Q-iv; R-i; S-iii
B. P-ii; Q-i; R-iv; S-iii
C. P-ii; Q-iv; R-iii; S-i
D. P-iii; Q-iv; R-i; S-ii
questions.to_find: Match C declarations/statements to their correct descriptions.

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of C storage classes and memory concepts. Each of P, Q, R, S represents a C statement or declaration, and you must match it to the correct description (i through iv). Understanding what static, register, pointer arrays, and memory leaks mean is essential.

keywords.explain: A variable declared with `static` is stored in the data segment (BSS/data section) of memory, not on the stack.
keywords.example: static char var; — lives in the data section, initialized to 0.

keywords.explain: When dynamically allocated memory is no longer reachable (pointer overwritten) but not freed, it is a lost memory block that cannot be freed.
keywords.example: m = malloc(10); m = NULL; — the allocated block is now lost.

keywords.explain: char *ptr[10] is an array of 10 char pointers — a sequence of memory locations that store addresses.
keywords.example: ptr[0] holds an address, ptr[1] holds another address, etc.

keywords.explain: The `register` keyword requests the compiler to store the variable in a CPU register for fast access.
keywords.example: register int i; — hints to store i in a register.

understand.visual_alt: Four C constructs (static variable, memory leak via NULL assignment, pointer array, register variable) matched to memory/CPU concepts.

given.aim: Identify the correct matching between C declarations and their memory/storage descriptions.

terms.meaning: A static local or global variable stored in the data section (not heap or stack).
terms.example: static int x = 5; is stored in BSS or initialized data segment.
terms.connects: Matches description ii: 'A variable located in data section of memory'.

terms.meaning: Allocates memory then immediately loses the pointer by setting it to NULL — a memory leak.
terms.example: The 10-byte block allocated by malloc is now unreachable and cannot be freed.
terms.connects: Matches description iv: 'A lost memory which cannot be freed'.

terms.meaning: Declares an array of 10 char pointers — each element is a memory address.
terms.example: ptr[0] through ptr[9] each store an address (pointer to char).
terms.connects: Matches description i: 'Sequence of memory locations to store addresses'.

terms.meaning: The `register` keyword requests the variable to be stored in a CPU register.
terms.example: register int i; — compiler may place i in a hardware register.
terms.connects: Matches description iii: 'Request to allocate a CPU register to store data'.

given.plan: Match each C construct to its description: static -> data section; malloc then NULL -> memory leak; array of pointers -> sequence of addresses; register -> CPU register request.

steps.title: Match P (static char var) to description
steps.apply: The `static` storage class causes a variable to be stored in the data segment (data section) of memory, not on the stack. Description ii says 'A variable located in data section of memory'. P matches ii.
steps.note: static variables have program lifetime and are zero-initialized by default.

steps.title: Match Q (m = malloc(10); m = NULL) to description
steps.apply: malloc(10) allocates 10 bytes on the heap. Then m = NULL overwrites the pointer without calling free(). The allocated memory is now unreachable — a classic memory leak. Description iv says 'A lost memory which cannot be freed'. Q matches iv.
steps.note: This is the definition of a memory leak in C.

steps.title: Match R (char *ptr[10]) to description
steps.apply: char *ptr[10] is an array of 10 pointers to char. Each element stores a memory address (pointer). Description i says 'Sequence of memory locations to store addresses'. R matches i.
steps.note: This is different from char (*ptr)[10] which is a pointer to an array.

steps.title: Match S (register last val) to description
steps.apply: The `register` storage class is a hint to the compiler to store the variable in a CPU register for faster access. Description iii says 'Request to allocate a CPU register to store data'. S matches iii.
steps.note: Modern compilers may ignore this hint and use their own register allocation.

solution.result: P-ii, Q-iv, R-i, S-iii. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: C storage classes: static (data section), register (CPU register hint), dynamic memory (heap via malloc/free). Memory leak occurs when allocated memory pointer is overwritten without freeing.

questions.to_find: The printed integer value of n after all increment and decrement operations.

understand.plain: The program starts with m=10 and applies pre- and post-increment and decrement operators. Pre-increment (++m) increments m first and then uses the value, while post-increment (m++) uses the current value first and then increments. After these operations, decrement and subtraction are applied, and the final value of n is printed.

keywords.explain: Increments m immediately and returns the new value.
keywords.example: If m=10, then n = ++m gives m=11, n=11.

keywords.explain: Returns the current value of m, then increments m.
keywords.example: If m=11, then n1 = m++ gives n1=11, m=12.
If n=10 and n1=10, then n -= n1 gives n=0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="460" height="200" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="40" class="lbl ink">Step-by-step trace:</text><text x="20" y="65" class="sub ink">Start:  m=10, n=?, n1=?</text><text x="20" y="87" class="sub ink">n = ++m   => m=11, n=11</text><text x="20" y="109" class="sub ink">n1 = m++  => n1=11, m=12</text><text x="20" y="131" class="sub ink">n--       => n=10</text><text x="20" y="153" class="sub ink">--n1      => n1=10</text><rect x="20" y="165" width="440" height="35" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="30" y="188" class="lbl ink">n -= n1   => n = 10 - 10 = 0; printf prints 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step table showing values of m, n, n1 after each statement in the program.

given.aim: Trace the values of m, n, n1 through each statement and find the final printed value of n.

terms.meaning: Pre-increment: m is incremented to 11, then n is assigned 11.
Post-increment: n1 is assigned current m (11), then m is incremented to 12.

given.plan: Apply each operation in sequence, tracking m, n, and n1 at every step. The final value of n after n -= n1 is the answer.

steps.title: Initialize and pre-increment
steps.apply: m starts at 10. n = ++m: m is incremented first: m = 11, then n = m = 11. State: m=11, n=11.

steps.title: Post-increment assignment
steps.apply: n1 = m++ : n1 gets the current value of m = 11, then m is incremented: m = 12. State: m=12, n=11, n1=11.
steps.note: Post-increment: value used first, then incremented.

steps.title: Subtract and print
steps.apply: n -= n1 : n = n - n1 = 10 - 10 = 0. printf prints 0. But the official answer to GATE 2017 Set 2 Q54 is 0. Checking options: A.-1 B.0 C.1 D.2. The answer is B. 0.

solution.result: The output of the program is 0. Answer: B. 0.

questions.formula_note: Pre-increment (++m) modifies before use; post-increment (m++) uses current value then increments. Careful tracing of each operator's sequential effect on variable state is required.

understand.plain: The function fun(n) has two loops. The first loop repeatedly divides n by 2 until it reaches 1, counting the number of divisions in j. The second loop does the same thing to j, counting in sum. Effectively, the first loop computes j = floor(log_2(n)), and the second loop computes sum = floor(log_2(j)).

keywords.explain: Repeatedly dividing n by 2 (integer division) until n=1 gives the number of times equal to floor(log_2(n)).
keywords.example: n=8: 8->4->3 iterations? No: 8->4->2->1, that is 3 divisions, and log_2(8)=3.

keywords.explain: The number of times you can divide n by 2 before reaching 1 equals floor(log_2(n)).
keywords.example: n=2^31: floor(log_2(2^31)) = 31. So j=31 after the first loop.
log_2(31) ≈ 4.95, so floor is 4. But the loop runs while j>1 so sum counts divisions.

understand.visual_alt: Two loops: first counts log_2(2^31)=31 giving j=31; second counts floor(log_2(31))=4 or 5 depending on exact loop behavior.

given.aim: Trace fun(2^31) through both loops to find the returned value sum.

terms.meaning: The input value; 2 raised to the power 31.
terms.connects: First loop runs while i > 1, dividing by 2 each time.

terms.term: First loop: j counts divisions of n
terms.meaning: j = number of times n is divided by 2 to reach 1, i.e., j = floor(log_2(n)).
terms.connects: j becomes the input to the second loop.

terms.term: Second loop: sum counts divisions of j
terms.meaning: sum = number of times j is divided by 2 to reach 1, i.e., sum = floor(log_2(j)).
terms.example: For j=31: 31->15->7->3->1, that is 4 divisions, sum=4. But loop condition is j>1.

given.plan: Compute j by tracing the first loop with n=2^31 (j = 31). Then trace the second loop with j=31 to find sum. Carefully count each iteration where j > 1.

steps.title: First loop: compute j for n = 2^31
steps.formula_raw: for (i = n; i > 1; i = i/2) j++
steps.apply: Starting with i = 2^31. Each iteration divides i by 2 and increments j. The loop runs while i > 1. i takes values: 2^31, 2^30, 2^29, ..., 2^1 = 2. When i=2, the condition i>1 is true, j is incremented, then i becomes 1. When i=1, condition fails. So j is incremented for i = 2^31, 2^30, ..., 2^1 which is 31 values. j = 31.
steps.note: The first loop counts floor(log_2(n)) = 31 for n = 2^31.

steps.title: Second loop: compute sum for j = 31
steps.note: The second loop runs 4 times for j=31, giving sum=4. But the answer choices suggest 5, so let me recheck.

steps.title: Recheck first loop boundary
steps.formula_raw: for (i = n; i > 1; i = i/2) j++
steps.apply: n = 2^31. i starts at 2^31. Loop: i>1? yes (2^31>1), j++, i=2^30. ... i=2? yes (2>1), j++, i=1. i=1? no (1>1 is false), stop. Iterations: 31. j = 31. Second loop with j=31: j=31>1: sum++, j=15. j=15>1: sum++, j=7. j=7>1: sum++, j=3. j=3>1: sum++, j=1. j=1: stop. sum=4. Answer should be 4, but option A=4. Wait, let me recheck the options from the image.
steps.note: From the next page image: A.4, B.5, C.6, D.40. So the answer is A.4.

steps.title: Final answer determination
steps.apply: j = floor(log_2(2^31)) = 31. sum = floor(log_2(31)). Since 2^4=16 < 31 < 32=2^5, we get floor(log_2(31)) = 4. So sum = 4. The correct answer is A. 4.
steps.note: The second loop terminates when j reaches 1, giving sum = 4 for j = 31.

solution.result: The value returned when fun(2^31) is called is 4. Answer: A. 4.

questions.formula_note: Each halving loop computes floor(log_2(n)) iterations. The function computes floor(log_2(floor(log_2(n)))). For n=2^31: first loop gives j=31, second loop gives sum = floor(log_2(31)) = 4.
